question:
4. In assigning dormitory rooms, a college gives preference to pairs of students in this order:
\[
A A, A B, A C, B B, B C, A D, C C, B D, C D, D D
\]
in which \( A A \) means two seniors, \( A B \) means a senior and a junior, etc. Determine numerical values to assign to \( A, B, C, D \) so that the set of numbers \( A \) \( +A, A+B, A+C, B+B \), etc., corresponding to the order above will be in descending magnitude. Find the general solution and the solution in least positive integers.
solution:
Solution. The inequalities to be solved are
\[
\begin{array}{c}
2 A>A+B>A+C>2 B>B+C>A+D> \\
2 C>B+D>C+D>2 D .
\end{array}
\]

Evidently we must have \( A>B>C>D \), so put
\[
A=\alpha+B . B=\beta+C . C=\gamma+D .
\]
where \( \alpha, \beta \) and \( \gamma \) are positive. The first, second, fourth, eighth, and ninth inequalities in (1) are now satisfied, while the third. fifth, sixth, and seventh inequalities become, respectively
\[
\alpha>\beta . \quad \gamma>\alpha . \quad \alpha+\beta>\gamma . \quad \text { and } \quad \gamma>\beta .
\]

The inequality \( \gamma>\beta \) is a consequence of \( \gamma>\alpha \) and \( \alpha>\beta \), so it can be dropped from this system. Now put \( \alpha=\beta+\delta, \gamma=\alpha+\epsilon \) where \( \delta \) and \( \epsilon \) are positive. Then \( \alpha+\beta>\gamma \) becomes \( \beta>\epsilon \), so we can put \( \beta=\epsilon+\zeta \) with !? positive. Then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
\alpha=\delta+\epsilon+\zeta \\
\beta=\epsilon+\zeta \\
\gamma=\delta+2 \epsilon+\zeta
\end{array}
\]
and finally.
\[
\begin{array}{l}
A=2 \delta+4 \epsilon+3 \zeta+D \\
B=\delta+3 \epsilon+2 \zeta+D \\
C=\delta+2 \epsilon+\zeta+D .
\end{array}
\]

Here \( D \) can be chosen arbitrarily, while \( \delta, \epsilon \), and \( \zeta \) must be positive. It follows either from the derivation or from a direct check that if \( A, B, C, D \) satisfy (2). where \( \delta, \epsilon \) and \( \zeta \) are positive. then they satisfy the inequalities (1).

To find integral solutions. we note that if we start with integral \( A . B . C \). \( D \) satisfying (1). then all the numbers \( \alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta, \epsilon, \zeta \) will be positive integers. Hence the least solution in positive integers is obtained by choosing \( \delta=\epsilon=\zeta=D=1 \). Hence \( A=10 . B=7, C=5, D=1 \) is the least solution in positive integers.

descriptive_long.question:
4. In assigning dormitory rooms, a college gives preference to pairs of students in this order:
\[
seniorv seniorv, seniorv juniorv, seniorv sophomv, juniorv juniorv, juniorv sophomv, seniorv freshmv, sophomv sophomv, juniorv freshmv, sophomv freshmv, freshmv freshmv
\]
in which \( seniorv seniorv \) means two seniors, \( seniorv juniorv \) means a senior and a junior, etc. Determine numerical values to assign to \( seniorv, juniorv, sophomv, freshmv \) so that the set of numbers \( seniorv+seniorv, seniorv+juniorv, seniorv+sophomv, juniorv+juniorv \), etc., corresponding to the order above will be in descending magnitude. Find the general solution and the solution in least positive integers.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The inequalities to be solved are
\[
\begin{array}{c}
2\,seniorv > seniorv + juniorv > seniorv + sophomv > 2\,juniorv > juniorv + sophomv > seniorv + freshmv > \\
2\,sophomv > juniorv + freshmv > sophomv + freshmv > 2\,freshmv .
\end{array}
\]

Evidently we must have \( seniorv > juniorv > sophomv > freshmv \), so put
\[
seniorv = greekal + juniorv, \quad
juniorv = greekbe + sophomv, \quad
sophomv = greekga + freshmv .
\]
where \( greekal, greekbe \) and \( greekga \) are positive. The first, second, fourth, eighth, and ninth inequalities in (1) are now satisfied, while the third, fifth, sixth, and seventh inequalities become, respectively
\[
greekal > greekbe, \quad greekga > greekal, \quad greekal + greekbe > greekga, \quad \text{and} \quad greekga > greekbe .
\]

The inequality \( greekga > greekbe \) is a consequence of \( greekga > greekal \) and \( greekal > greekbe \), so it can be dropped from this system. Now put \( greekal = greekbe + greekde, \quad greekga = greekal + greekep \) where \( greekde \) and \( greekep \) are positive. Then \( greekal + greekbe > greekga \) becomes \( greekbe > greekep \), so we can put \( greekbe = greekep + greekze \) with \( greekze \) positive. Then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
greekal = greekde + greekep + greekze ,\\
greekbe = greekep + greekze ,\\
greekga = greekde + 2\,greekep + greekze .
\end{array}
\]
and finally.
\[
\begin{array}{l}
seniorv = 2\,greekde + 4\,greekep + 3\,greekze + freshmv ,\\
juniorv = greekde + 3\,greekep + 2\,greekze + freshmv ,\\
sophomv = greekde + 2\,greekep + greekze + freshmv .
\end{array}
\]

Here \( freshmv \) can be chosen arbitrarily, while \( greekde, greekep \), and \( greekze \) must be positive. It follows either from the derivation or from a direct check that if \( seniorv, juniorv, sophomv, freshmv \) satisfy (2), where \( greekde, greekep \) and \( greekze \) are positive, then they satisfy the inequalities (1).

To find integral solutions, we note that if we start with integral \( seniorv, juniorv, sophomv, freshmv \) satisfying (1), then all the numbers \( greekal, greekbe, greekga, greekde, greekep, greekze \) will be positive integers. Hence the least solution in positive integers is obtained by choosing \( greekde = greekep = greekze = freshmv = 1 \). Hence \( seniorv = 10, juniorv = 7, sophomv = 5, freshmv = 1 \) is the least solution in positive integers.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
4. In assigning dormitory rooms, a college gives preference to pairs of students in this order:
\[
copperhead\ copperhead, \, copperhead\ laundromat, \, copperhead\ nightshade, \, laundromat\ laundromat, \, laundromat\ nightshade, \, copperhead\ flashpoint, \, nightshade\ nightshade, \, laundromat\ flashpoint, \, nightshade\ flashpoint, \, flashpoint\ flashpoint
\]
in which \( copperhead\ copperhead \) means two seniors, \( copperhead\ laundromat \) means a senior and a junior, etc. Determine numerical values to assign to \( copperhead,\, laundromat,\, nightshade,\, flashpoint \) so that the set of numbers \( copperhead + copperhead,\, copperhead+laundromat,\, copperhead+nightshade,\, laundromat+laundromat \), etc., corresponding to the order above will be in descending magnitude. Find the general solution and the solution in least positive integers.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The inequalities to be solved are
\[
\begin{array}{c}
2\,copperhead > copperhead+laundromat > copperhead+nightshade > 2\,laundromat > laundromat+nightshade > copperhead+flashpoint > \\
2\,nightshade > laundromat+flashpoint > nightshade+flashpoint > 2\,flashpoint .
\end{array}
\]

Evidently we must have \( copperhead>laundromat>nightshade>flashpoint \), so put
\[
copperhead=playground+laundromat .\; laundromat=seashells+nightshade .\; nightshade=sunflower+flashpoint .
\]
where \( playground, seashells \) and \( sunflower \) are positive. The first, second, fourth, eighth, and ninth inequalities in (1) are now satisfied, while the third, fifth, sixth, and seventh inequalities become, respectively
\[
playground>seashells . \quad sunflower>playground . \quad playground+seashells>sunflower . \quad \text { and } \quad sunflower>seashells .
\]

The inequality \( sunflower>seashells \) is a consequence of \( sunflower>playground \) and \( playground>seashells \), so it can be dropped from this system. Now put \( playground=seashells+rainstorm, \; sunflower=playground+stargazer \) where \( rainstorm \) and \( stargazer \) are positive. Then \( playground+seashells>sunflower \) becomes \( seashells>stargazer \), so we can put \( seashells=stargazer+moonlight \) with !? positive. Then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
playground=rainstorm+stargazer+moonlight \\
seashells=stargazer+moonlight \\
sunflower=rainstorm+2\,stargazer+moonlight
\end{array}
\]
and finally.
\[
\begin{array}{l}
copperhead=2\,rainstorm+4\,stargazer+3\,moonlight+flashpoint \\
laundromat=rainstorm+3\,stargazer+2\,moonlight+flashpoint \\
nightshade=rainstorm+2\,stargazer+moonlight+flashpoint .
\end{array}
\]

Here \( flashpoint \) can be chosen arbitrarily, while \( rainstorm, stargazer \), and \( moonlight \) must be positive. It follows either from the derivation or from a direct check that if \( copperhead, laundromat, nightshade, flashpoint \) satisfy (2), where \( rainstorm, stargazer \) and \( moonlight \) are positive, then they satisfy the inequalities (1).

To find integral solutions, we note that if we start with integral \( copperhead, laundromat, nightshade, flashpoint \) satisfying (1), then all the numbers \( playground, seashells, sunflower, rainstorm, stargazer, moonlight \) will be positive integers. Hence the least solution in positive integers is obtained by choosing \( rainstorm=stargazer=moonlight=flashpoint=1 \). Hence \( copperhead=10,\; laundromat=7,\; nightshade=5,\; flashpoint=1 \) is the least solution in positive integers.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
4. In assigning dormitory rooms, a college gives preference to pairs of students in this order:
\[
freshman freshman, freshman seniorly, freshman graduate, seniorly seniorly, seniorly graduate, freshman upperclass, graduate graduate, seniorly upperclass, graduate upperclass, upperclass upperclass
\]
in which \( freshman freshman \) means two seniors, \( freshman seniorly \) means a senior and a junior, etc. Determine numerical values to assign to \( freshman, seniorly, graduate, upperclass \) so that the set of numbers \( freshman \) \( +freshman, freshman+seniorly, freshman+graduate, seniorly+seniorly \), etc., corresponding to the order above will be in descending magnitude. Find the general solution and the solution in least positive integers.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The inequalities to be solved are
\[
\begin{array}{c}
2 freshman>freshman+seniorly>freshman+graduate>2 seniorly>seniorly+graduate>freshman+upperclass> \\
2 graduate>seniorly+upperclass>graduate+upperclass>2 upperclass .
\end{array}
\]

Evidently we must have \( freshman>seniorly>graduate>upperclass \), so put
\[
freshman=omegaend+seniorly .\; seniorly=alphabeg+graduate .\; graduate=deltamid+upperclass .
\]
where \( omegaend, alphabeg \) and \( deltamid \) are positive. The first, second, fourth, eighth, and ninth inequalities in (1) are now satisfied, while the third. fifth, sixth, and seventh inequalities become, respectively
\[
omegaend>alphabeg . \quad deltamid>omegaend . \quad omegaend+alphabeg>deltamid . \quad \text { and } \quad deltamid>alphabeg .
\]

The inequality \( deltamid>alphabeg \) is a consequence of \( deltamid>omegaend \) and \( omegaend>alphabeg \), so it can be dropped from this system. Now put \( omegaend=alphabeg+staticval, \; deltamid=omegaend+largesize \) where \( staticval \) and \( largesize \) are positive. Then \( omegaend+alphabeg>deltamid \) becomes \( alphabeg>largesize \), so we can put \( alphabeg=largesize+concrete \) with !? positive. Then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
omegaend=staticval+largesize+concrete \\
alphabeg=largesize+concrete \\
deltamid=staticval+2 largesize+concrete
\end{array}
\]
and finally.
\[
\begin{array}{l}
freshman=2 staticval+4 largesize+3 concrete+upperclass \\
seniorly=staticval+3 largesize+2 concrete+upperclass \\
graduate=staticval+2 largesize+concrete+upperclass .
\end{array}
\]

Here \( upperclass \) can be chosen arbitrarily, while \( staticval, largesize \), and \( concrete \) must be positive. It follows either from the derivation or from a direct check that if \( freshman, seniorly, graduate, upperclass \) satisfy (2). where \( staticval, largesize \) and \( concrete \) are positive. then they satisfy the inequalities (1).

To find integral solutions. we note that if we start with integral \( freshman . seniorly . graduate \). \( upperclass \) satisfying (1). then all the numbers \( omegaend, alphabeg, deltamid, staticval, largesize, concrete \) will be positive integers. Hence the least solution in positive integers is obtained by choosing \( staticval=largesize=concrete=upperclass=1 \). Hence \( freshman=10 . seniorly=7, graduate=5, upperclass=1 \) is the least solution in positive integers.

garbled_string.question:
4. In assigning dormitory rooms, a college gives preference to pairs of students in this order:
\[
qzxwvtnp qzxwvtnp, qzxwvtnp hjgrksla, qzxwvtnp mfldepor, hjgrksla hjgrksla, hjgrksla mfldepor, qzxwvtnp vknsqiut, mfldepor mfldepor, hjgrksla vknsqiut, mfldepor vknsqiut, vknsqiut vknsqiut
\]
in which \( qzxwvtnp qzxwvtnp \) means two seniors, \( qzxwvtnp hjgrksla \) means a senior and a junior, etc. Determine numerical values to assign to \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mfldepor, vknsqiut \) so that the set of numbers \( qzxwvtnp \) \( +qzxwvtnp, qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla, qzxwvtnp+mfldepor, hjgrksla+hjgrksla \), etc., corresponding to the order above will be in descending magnitude. Find the general solution and the solution in least positive integers.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The inequalities to be solved are
\[
\begin{array}{c}
2 qzxwvtnp>qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla>qzxwvtnp+mfldepor>2 hjgrksla>hjgrksla+mfldepor>qzxwvtnp+vknsqiut> \\
2 mfldepor>hjgrksla+vknsqiut>mfldepor+vknsqiut>2 vknsqiut .
\end{array}
\]

Evidently we must have \( qzxwvtnp>hjgrksla>mfldepor>vknsqiut \), so put
\[
qzxwvtnp=pzocrine+hjgrksla . \quad hjgrksla=svakudom+mfldepor . \quad mfldepor=welfsibn+vknsqiut .
\]
where \( pzocrine, svakudom \) and \( welfsibn \) are positive. The first, second, fourth, eighth, and ninth inequalities in (1) are now satisfied, while the third. fifth, sixth, and seventh inequalities become, respectively
\[
pzocrine>svakudom . \quad welfsibn>pzocrine . \quad pzocrine+svakudom>welfsibn . \quad \text { and } \quad welfsibn>svakudom .
\]

The inequality \( welfsibn>svakudom \) is a consequence of \( welfsibn>pzocrine \) and \( pzocrine>svakudom \), so it can be dropped from this system. Now put \( pzocrine=svakudom+kluvemta, welfsibn=pzocrine+draximop \) where \( kluvemta \) and \( draximop \) are positive. Then \( pzocrine+svakudom>welfsibn \) becomes \( svakudom>draximop \), so we can put \( svakudom=draximop+nurbisyq \) with nurbisyq positive. Then
\[
\begin{array}{c}
pzocrine=kluvemta+draximop+nurbisyq \\
svakudom=draximop+nurbisyq \\
welfsibn=kluvemta+2 draximop+nurbisyq
\end{array}
\]
and finally.
\[
\begin{array}{l}
qzxwvtnp=2 kluvemta+4 draximop+3 nurbisyq+vknsqiut \\
hjgrksla=kluvemta+3 draximop+2 nurbisyq+vknsqiut \\
mfldepor=kluvemta+2 draximop+nurbisyq+vknsqiut .
\end{array}
\]

Here \( vknsqiut \) can be chosen arbitrarily, while \( kluvemta, draximop \), and \( nurbisyq \) must be positive. It follows either from the derivation or from a direct check that if \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mfldepor, vknsqiut \) satisfy (2), where \( kluvemta, draximop \) and \( nurbisyq \) are positive, then they satisfy the inequalities (1).

To find integral solutions, we note that if we start with integral \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, mfldepor, vknsqiut \) satisfying (1), then all the numbers \( pzocrine, svakudom, welfsibn, kluvemta, draximop, nurbisyq \) will be positive integers. Hence the least solution in positive integers is obtained by choosing \( kluvemta=draximop=nurbisyq=vknsqiut=1 \). Hence \( qzxwvtnp=10 . hjgrksla=7, mfldepor=5, vknsqiut=1 \) is the least solution in positive integers.

kernel_variant.question:
In an intramural mathematics contest the five collegiate categories  

Senior, Junior, Sophomore, Freshman, Pre-Freshman  

are to be awarded, in this order, distinct positive even integers  

P > Q > R > S > T.  

A two-student team receives a rating equal to the sum of the points of its members.  
The organising committee demands that the fifteen possible (unordered) team-types have
strictly decreasing ratings in the sequence  
 PP, PQ, PR, QQ, PS, QR, RR, QS, PT, RS, SS, QT, RT, ST, TT. (\star )

(Thus, for instance, a team of one Junior and one Sophomore---type QR---must outrank any
team of two Sophomores---type RR.)

1.  Determine all quintuples (P,Q,R,S,T) of distinct positive even integers that realise ordering (\star ).  
2.  Find the unique quintuple for which the sum P+Q+R+S+T is minimal.
kernel_variant.solution:
Write  
 P > Q > R > S > T  (all positive even).  

Introduce the positive even gaps  

 \alpha  = P-Q, \beta  = Q-R, \gamma  = R-S, \delta  = S-T   (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ,\delta  \in  2\mathbb{N}).  (1)

Hence  

 P = T + \delta  + \gamma  + \beta  + \alpha ,  
 Q = T + \delta  + \gamma  + \beta ,  
 R = T + \delta  + \gamma ,  
 S = T + \delta .  (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  Translating the fifteen inequalities
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Ordering (\star ) is equivalent to the chain  

2P > P+Q > P+R > 2Q > P+S > Q+R > 2R  
   > Q+S > P+T > R+S > 2S > Q+T > R+T > S+T > 2T.  (3)

Substituting (2) and cancelling the common addend T gives the seven independent
conditions  

(a) \alpha  > \beta ,   (b) \alpha  > \gamma ,   (c) \gamma  > \beta ,  
(d) \alpha  < \beta +\gamma , (e) \delta  > \alpha ,  (f) \delta  > \beta +\gamma ,  (g) \delta  < \alpha +\beta .  (4)

Because all variables are even, ``>'' means ``at least 2 larger''.
Conversely, if (4) holds then (3) (hence (\star )) holds, so (4) is
necessary and sufficient.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  General parametrisation
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Choose any positive even integers \alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ,\delta ,T satisfying (4) and define
P,Q,R,S by (2).  The resulting quintuple (P,Q,R,S,T) consists of distinct
positive even integers fulfilling (\star ).  Conversely, every quintuple that
realises (\star ) yields a unique 5-tuple (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ,\delta ,T) with (4).  Therefore

 All admissible quintuples arise from (4) and (2). \square 

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Minimising the total \Sigma  = P+Q+R+S+T
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put  

 \Sigma  = P+Q+R+S+T = 5T + F, where F = 4\delta  + 3\gamma  + 2\beta  + \alpha . (5)

Since all numbers are even, the smallest possible base value is T = 2,
so we must minimise F subject to (4).

For convenience write every even number as twice a positive integer:
 \alpha  = 2a, \beta  = 2b, \gamma  = 2c, \delta  = 2d with a,b,c,d \in  \mathbb{N} and  
 a > b, a > c, c > b, a < b+c, d > a, d > b+c, d < a+b  (6)

We now determine the least quadruple (b,c,a,d).

------------------------------------------------
3.1  A lower bound on \beta   (=2b)
------------------------------------------------
*  b = 1 (\beta  = 2)  
 Then c \geq  2 (\gamma  \geq  4), a \geq  3 (\alpha  \geq  6) and d \geq  a+1 \geq  4.  
 But d < a+b \leq  4, contradiction.  
*  b = 2 (\beta  = 4)  
 We need c \geq  3.  Put the smallest c = 3 (\gamma  = 6).  
 Then a must satisfy a > c and a < b+c, hence a = 4 (\alpha  = 8) is forced.  
 The d-conditions give  
  d > a = 4  and  d > b+c = 5 \Rightarrow  d \geq  6,  
  d < a+b = 6         \Rightarrow  d \leq  5, contradiction.  
Hence b \geq  3, i.e. \beta  \geq  6.

------------------------------------------------
3.2  Fixing \beta  = 6  (b = 3) and minimising \gamma 
------------------------------------------------
With b = 3 we must have c \geq  4 (\gamma  \geq  8).

------------------------------------------------
3.3  Try c = 4  (\gamma  = 8) and minimise \alpha 
------------------------------------------------
For c = 4 the inequalities demand
 a > c \Rightarrow  a \geq  5,  
 a < b+c = 7 \Rightarrow  a \in  {5,6}.  

*  a = 5  (\alpha  = 10)  
 d > a \Rightarrow  d \geq  6,  
 d > b+c = 7 \Rightarrow  d \geq  8,  
 d < a+b = 8 \Rightarrow  d \leq  7, impossible.

*  a = 6  (\alpha  = 12)  
 d > a = 6    \Rightarrow  d \geq  7,  
 d > b+c = 7  \Rightarrow  d \geq  8,  
 d < a+b = 9  \Rightarrow  d \leq  8, so d = 8 is forced.

The quadruple (b,c,a,d) = (3,4,6,8) --- equivalently  
 \beta  = 6, \gamma  = 8, \alpha  = 12, \delta  = 16 --- satisfies all of (6).

------------------------------------------------
3.4  No smaller F is possible
------------------------------------------------
Any increase in b, or in c, or in a, or in d raises at least one of the
coefficients 2\beta ,3\gamma ,\alpha ,4\delta  in F, so the above choice yields the absolute
minimum of F.

Thus
 F_min = 4\cdot 16 + 3\cdot 8 + 2\cdot 6 + 12 = 112,  
 \Sigma _min = 5\cdot 2 + 112 = 122.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  The unique minimal quintuple
--------------------------------------------------------------------
With T = 2 and (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ,\delta ) = (12,6,8,16) formula (2) gives  

 P = 44, Q = 32, R = 26, S = 18, T = 2.

Hence the minimal quintuple is (44,32,26,18,2) and its total is 122.
Uniqueness follows from the uniqueness of (b,c,a,d) obtained in 3.3.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  All admissible quintuples are obtained by choosing positive even
    \alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ,\delta ,T satisfying
      \alpha  > \beta , \alpha  > \gamma , \gamma  > \beta , \alpha  < \beta +\gamma ,
      \delta  > \alpha , \delta  > \beta +\gamma , \delta  < \alpha +\beta 
    and then setting  
  P = T+\delta +\gamma +\beta +\alpha , Q = T+\delta +\gamma +\beta , R = T+\delta +\gamma , S = T+\delta .

2.  The unique quintuple with minimal sum is  
  (P,Q,R,S,T) = (44, 32, 26, 18, 2), whose total is 122.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• More variables: the problem involves five unknowns instead of four, creating
  fifteen rather than ten team-types and considerably enlarging the system of
  inequalities.  

• Additional constraints: the numbers must be even, and the imposed order is
  not monotone in class seniority, so several inequalities are non-trivial.  

• Structural depth: after a change of variables the problem reduces to seven
  interdependent inequalities in four parameters, whose feasibility region is
  pieced together by analysing sharp lower and upper bounds.  

• Solution technique: obtaining the general solution requires recursive
  parameterisation, inequality chaining, and optimisation within an integer
  lattice – substantially more involved than the linear telescoping used in
  the original.  

• Minimality: proving uniqueness of the minimal even solution forces a
  careful minimisation of a weighted linear form subject to intricate parity
  and ordering constraints, again a step beyond the original exercise.

original_kernel_variant.question:
In an intramural mathematics contest the five collegiate categories  

Senior, Junior, Sophomore, Freshman, Pre-Freshman  

are to be awarded, in this order, distinct positive even integers  

P > Q > R > S > T.  

A two-student team receives a rating equal to the sum of the points of its members.  
The organising committee demands that the fifteen possible (unordered) team-types have
strictly decreasing ratings in the sequence  
 PP, PQ, PR, QQ, PS, QR, RR, QS, PT, RS, SS, QT, RT, ST, TT. (\star )

(Thus, for instance, a team of one Junior and one Sophomore---type QR---must outrank any
team of two Sophomores---type RR.)

1.  Determine all quintuples (P,Q,R,S,T) of distinct positive even integers that realise ordering (\star ).  
2.  Find the unique quintuple for which the sum P+Q+R+S+T is minimal.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Write  
 P > Q > R > S > T  (all positive even).  

Introduce the positive even gaps  

 \alpha  = P-Q, \beta  = Q-R, \gamma  = R-S, \delta  = S-T   (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ,\delta  \in  2\mathbb{N}).  (1)

Hence  

 P = T + \delta  + \gamma  + \beta  + \alpha ,  
 Q = T + \delta  + \gamma  + \beta ,  
 R = T + \delta  + \gamma ,  
 S = T + \delta .  (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  Translating the fifteen inequalities
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Ordering (\star ) is equivalent to the chain  

2P > P+Q > P+R > 2Q > P+S > Q+R > 2R  
   > Q+S > P+T > R+S > 2S > Q+T > R+T > S+T > 2T.  (3)

Substituting (2) and cancelling the common addend T gives the seven independent
conditions  

(a) \alpha  > \beta ,   (b) \alpha  > \gamma ,   (c) \gamma  > \beta ,  
(d) \alpha  < \beta +\gamma , (e) \delta  > \alpha ,  (f) \delta  > \beta +\gamma ,  (g) \delta  < \alpha +\beta .  (4)

Because all variables are even, ``>'' means ``at least 2 larger''.
Conversely, if (4) holds then (3) (hence (\star )) holds, so (4) is
necessary and sufficient.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  General parametrisation
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Choose any positive even integers \alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ,\delta ,T satisfying (4) and define
P,Q,R,S by (2).  The resulting quintuple (P,Q,R,S,T) consists of distinct
positive even integers fulfilling (\star ).  Conversely, every quintuple that
realises (\star ) yields a unique 5-tuple (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ,\delta ,T) with (4).  Therefore

 All admissible quintuples arise from (4) and (2). \square 

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Minimising the total \Sigma  = P+Q+R+S+T
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put  

 \Sigma  = P+Q+R+S+T = 5T + F, where F = 4\delta  + 3\gamma  + 2\beta  + \alpha . (5)

Since all numbers are even, the smallest possible base value is T = 2,
so we must minimise F subject to (4).

For convenience write every even number as twice a positive integer:
 \alpha  = 2a, \beta  = 2b, \gamma  = 2c, \delta  = 2d with a,b,c,d \in  \mathbb{N} and  
 a > b, a > c, c > b, a < b+c, d > a, d > b+c, d < a+b  (6)

We now determine the least quadruple (b,c,a,d).

------------------------------------------------
3.1  A lower bound on \beta   (=2b)
------------------------------------------------
*  b = 1 (\beta  = 2)  
 Then c \geq  2 (\gamma  \geq  4), a \geq  3 (\alpha  \geq  6) and d \geq  a+1 \geq  4.  
 But d < a+b \leq  4, contradiction.  
*  b = 2 (\beta  = 4)  
 We need c \geq  3.  Put the smallest c = 3 (\gamma  = 6).  
 Then a must satisfy a > c and a < b+c, hence a = 4 (\alpha  = 8) is forced.  
 The d-conditions give  
  d > a = 4  and  d > b+c = 5 \Rightarrow  d \geq  6,  
  d < a+b = 6         \Rightarrow  d \leq  5, contradiction.  
Hence b \geq  3, i.e. \beta  \geq  6.

------------------------------------------------
3.2  Fixing \beta  = 6  (b = 3) and minimising \gamma 
------------------------------------------------
With b = 3 we must have c \geq  4 (\gamma  \geq  8).

------------------------------------------------
3.3  Try c = 4  (\gamma  = 8) and minimise \alpha 
------------------------------------------------
For c = 4 the inequalities demand
 a > c \Rightarrow  a \geq  5,  
 a < b+c = 7 \Rightarrow  a \in  {5,6}.  

*  a = 5  (\alpha  = 10)  
 d > a \Rightarrow  d \geq  6,  
 d > b+c = 7 \Rightarrow  d \geq  8,  
 d < a+b = 8 \Rightarrow  d \leq  7, impossible.

*  a = 6  (\alpha  = 12)  
 d > a = 6    \Rightarrow  d \geq  7,  
 d > b+c = 7  \Rightarrow  d \geq  8,  
 d < a+b = 9  \Rightarrow  d \leq  8, so d = 8 is forced.

The quadruple (b,c,a,d) = (3,4,6,8) --- equivalently  
 \beta  = 6, \gamma  = 8, \alpha  = 12, \delta  = 16 --- satisfies all of (6).

------------------------------------------------
3.4  No smaller F is possible
------------------------------------------------
Any increase in b, or in c, or in a, or in d raises at least one of the
coefficients 2\beta ,3\gamma ,\alpha ,4\delta  in F, so the above choice yields the absolute
minimum of F.

Thus
 F_min = 4\cdot 16 + 3\cdot 8 + 2\cdot 6 + 12 = 112,  
 \Sigma _min = 5\cdot 2 + 112 = 122.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 4.  The unique minimal quintuple
--------------------------------------------------------------------
With T = 2 and (\alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ,\delta ) = (12,6,8,16) formula (2) gives  

 P = 44, Q = 32, R = 26, S = 18, T = 2.

Hence the minimal quintuple is (44,32,26,18,2) and its total is 122.
Uniqueness follows from the uniqueness of (b,c,a,d) obtained in 3.3.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  All admissible quintuples are obtained by choosing positive even
    \alpha ,\beta ,\gamma ,\delta ,T satisfying
      \alpha  > \beta , \alpha  > \gamma , \gamma  > \beta , \alpha  < \beta +\gamma ,
      \delta  > \alpha , \delta  > \beta +\gamma , \delta  < \alpha +\beta 
    and then setting  
  P = T+\delta +\gamma +\beta +\alpha , Q = T+\delta +\gamma +\beta , R = T+\delta +\gamma , S = T+\delta .

2.  The unique quintuple with minimal sum is  
  (P,Q,R,S,T) = (44, 32, 26, 18, 2), whose total is 122.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• More variables: the problem involves five unknowns instead of four, creating
  fifteen rather than ten team-types and considerably enlarging the system of
  inequalities.  

• Additional constraints: the numbers must be even, and the imposed order is
  not monotone in class seniority, so several inequalities are non-trivial.  

• Structural depth: after a change of variables the problem reduces to seven
  interdependent inequalities in four parameters, whose feasibility region is
  pieced together by analysing sharp lower and upper bounds.  

• Solution technique: obtaining the general solution requires recursive
  parameterisation, inequality chaining, and optimisation within an integer
  lattice – substantially more involved than the linear telescoping used in
  the original.  

• Minimality: proving uniqueness of the minimal even solution forces a
  careful minimisation of a weighted linear form subject to intricate parity
  and ordering constraints, again a step beyond the original exercise.
